news_summary: Recent health breakthroughs reveal safer natural treatments, improved oral care preserving beneficial bacteria, deeper insights into obesity-related health risks by gender, and the significant impact of genetics on lifespan.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées dans le domaine de la santé mettent en lumière des traitements naturels plus sûrs, des soins bucco-dentaires améliorés qui préservent les bactéries bénéfiques, une meilleure compréhension des risques pour la santé liés à l'obésité selon le sexe, ainsi que l'impact significatif de la génétique sur la durée de vie.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud revelan tratamientos naturales más seguros, una mejor higiene bucal que preserva las bacterias beneficiosas, un conocimiento más profundo de los riesgos para la salud relacionados con la obesidad según el género y el importante impacto de la genética en la esperanza de vida.

articles.title: Stanford scientists discover “natural Ozempic” without side effects
articles.summary: A newly discovered molecule could reshape the future of weight loss treatments by mimicking the powerful appetite-suppressing effects of drugs like Ozempic — but without many of the unpleasant side effects. Identified using artificial intelligence, this tiny peptide, called BRP, appears to act directly on the brain’s appetite-control center, helping animals eat less and lose fat without nausea or muscle loss.
articles.content:
Scientists at Stanford Medicine have identified a naturally occurring molecule that appears to mimic some of the weight loss effects of semaglutide, the drug widely known as Ozempic. In animal studies, the molecule reduced appetite and body weight while avoiding several common side effects such as nausea, constipation, and muscle loss.

The molecule, called BRP, works through a different but related biological pathway and activates distinct groups of neurons in the brain. This suggests it may offer a more precise way to control appetite and metabolism.

"The receptors targeted by semaglutide are found in the brain but also in the gut, pancreas and other tissues," said assistant professor of pathology Katrin Svensson, PhD. "That's why Ozempic has widespread effects including slowing the movement of food through the digestive tract and lowering blood sugar levels. In contrast, BRP appears to act specifically in the hypothalamus, which controls appetite and metabolism."

Svensson, the senior author of the study published in Nature, has also co-founded a company that plans to begin human clinical trials in the near future. The study's lead author is senior research scientist Laetitia Coassolo, PhD.

How Artificial Intelligence Led to the Discovery

The discovery relied heavily on artificial intelligence to sort through a large group of molecules known as prohormones. These molecules are initially inactive but can be cut into smaller fragments called peptides, some of which function as hormones that influence processes like metabolism in the brain and body.

Because each prohormone can be split in many different ways, identifying useful peptide hormones using traditional lab methods is extremely challenging. Researchers often struggle to distinguish these rare signaling molecules from the many inactive fragments created during normal protein breakdown.

To narrow the search, the team focused on an enzyme called prohormone convertase 1/3, which cuts proteins at specific locations and has been linked to obesity. One well-known product of this process is glucagon-like peptide 1, or GLP-1, which helps regulate appetite and blood sugar. Semaglutide works by mimicking GLP-1.

"Peptide Predictor" Identifies Thousands of Candidates

Instead of relying on traditional protein analysis methods, the researchers developed a computer tool called Peptide Predictor. This algorithm scanned all 20,000 human protein-coding genes to identify where prohormones could be cut into peptides.

The team then narrowed their focus to proteins that are secreted outside cells, a key feature of hormones, and that contain multiple potential cleavage points. This reduced the list to 373 prohormones suitable for further testing.

"The algorithm was absolutely key to our findings," Svensson said.

From these proteins, the system predicted 2,683 possible peptides. Researchers selected 100 of them, including GLP-1, and tested how they affected lab-grown brain cells.

A Tiny Peptide With Powerful Effects

As expected, GLP-1 significantly increased activity in the neurons. However, one much smaller peptide, made up of only 12 amino acids, produced an even stronger response, boosting activity tenfold compared to control cells.

This peptide was named BRP, after its parent molecule BPM/retinoic acid inducible neural specific 2, or BRINP2 (BRINP2-related-peptide).

Animal Studies Show Reduced Appetite and Fat Loss

When tested in lean mice and minipigs (which more closely mirror human metabolism and eating patterns than mice do), BRP significantly reduced food intake. A single injection before feeding lowered consumption by up to 50% within an hour.

In obese mice, daily injections over 14 days led to an average weight loss of 3 grams, primarily from fat. In contrast, untreated mice gained about 3 grams during the same period. The treated animals also showed improvements in glucose and insulin tolerance.

Importantly, the animals did not show changes in movement, water intake, anxiety-like behavior, or digestion. Additional analyses confirmed that BRP works through different brain and metabolic pathways than GLP-1 or semaglutide.

A More Targeted Approach to Weight Loss

The researchers are now working to identify the specific receptors that interact with BRP and to better understand how it functions in the body. They are also exploring ways to extend its effects so it could be used more conveniently if it proves effective in people.

"The lack of effective drugs to treat obesity in humans has been a problem for decades," Svensson said. "Nothing we've tested before has compared to semaglutide's ability to decrease appetite and body weight. We are very eager to learn if it is safe and effective in humans."

Collaboration and Funding

The research involved scientists from the University of California, Berkeley; the University of Minnesota; and the University of British Columbia. Funding came from the National Institutes of Health (grants R01DK125260, P30DK116074, K99AR081618 and GM113854), along with several Stanford programs, the American Heart Association, the Carlsberg Foundation, and the Wu Tsai Human Performance Alliance.

Svensson and Coassolo are listed as inventors on patents related to BRP peptides for metabolic disorders, and Svensson is a co-founder of Merrifield Therapeutics.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a natural molecule (BRP) that mimics the appetite-suppressing effects of a widely used weight loss drug (Ozempic) but without common side effects. This breakthrough has broad implications for obesity treatment, a major public health issue, and is supported by detailed research and planned human trials, indicating substantial positive real-world impact and significance.
articles.personality_title: Stanford scientists find natural molecule that reduces appetite without side effects
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Obesity is a major health issue, and medicines like Ozempic help people lose weight by reducing appetite. However, these drugs often cause side effects such as nausea and muscle loss.

**What happened** – Researchers at Stanford discovered a natural molecule called BRP that lowers appetite and body weight in animals without the usual side effects. Using artificial intelligence, they identified BRP as a small peptide that acts directly on the brain’s appetite center, unlike Ozempic which affects many parts of the body.

**Impact** – BRP works differently from current drugs and targets the hypothalamus, the part of the brain controlling hunger and metabolism. In studies with mice and minipigs, BRP cut food intake by up to half and helped obese mice lose fat without causing sickness or muscle loss. This finding could lead to safer, more precise treatments for obesity.

**What's next step** – The team plans to start human clinical trials soon to test if BRP is safe and effective in people. They also want to understand how BRP works in the body and find ways to make it last longer for easier use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found a natural molecule that can reduce appetite and body fat without side effects, offering a promising new approach to weight loss treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques de Stanford découvrent une molécule naturelle qui réduit l'appétit sans effets secondaires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'obésité est un problème de santé important, et des médicaments comme Ozempic aident à perdre du poids en réduisant l'appétit. Cependant, ces traitements provoquent souvent des effets secondaires comme des nausées et une perte musculaire.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de Stanford ont découvert une molécule naturelle appelée BRP qui diminue l'appétit et le poids chez les animaux sans ces effets secondaires. Grâce à l'intelligence artificielle, ils ont identifié BRP comme un petit peptide qui agit directement sur le centre de l'appétit dans le cerveau, contrairement à Ozempic qui affecte plusieurs parties du corps.

**Impact** – BRP fonctionne différemment des médicaments actuels en ciblant l'hypothalamus, la zone du cerveau qui contrôle la faim et le métabolisme. Chez les souris et les mini-porcs, BRP a réduit la consommation de nourriture jusqu'à 50 % et aidé les souris obèses à perdre de la graisse sans provoquer de malaise ni de perte musculaire. Cette découverte pourrait permettre des traitements de l'obésité plus sûrs et plus précis.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe prévoit bientôt de commencer des essais cliniques sur des humains pour vérifier si BRP est sûr et efficace. Ils veulent aussi mieux comprendre son fonctionnement et trouver des moyens de prolonger ses effets pour faciliter son utilisation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé une molécule naturelle capable de réduire l'appétit et la graisse corporelle sans effets secondaires, ouvrant une nouvelle voie prometteuse pour le traitement de la perte de poids.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos de Stanford descubren molécula natural que reduce el apetito sin efectos secundarios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La obesidad es un problema de salud importante, y medicamentos como Ozempic ayudan a perder peso al reducir el apetito. Sin embargo, estos fármacos suelen causar efectos secundarios como náuseas y pérdida muscular.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Stanford encontraron una molécula natural llamada BRP que reduce el apetito y el peso en animales sin los efectos secundarios comunes. Usaron inteligencia artificial para identificar a BRP como un pequeño péptido que actúa directamente en el centro del apetito en el cerebro, a diferencia de Ozempic que afecta varias partes del cuerpo.

**Impacto** – BRP funciona de manera diferente a los medicamentos actuales, enfocándose en el hipotálamo, la parte del cerebro que controla el hambre y el metabolismo. En estudios con ratones y minicerdos, BRP redujo la comida hasta en un 50 % y ayudó a ratones obesos a perder grasa sin causar enfermedad o pérdida muscular. Esto podría llevar a tratamientos para la obesidad más seguros y específicos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea iniciar ensayos clínicos en humanos para probar si BRP es seguro y efectivo. También buscan entender mejor cómo funciona y cómo hacer que dure más para facilitar su uso.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos han encontrado una molécula natural que puede reducir el apetito y la grasa corporal sin efectos secundarios, ofreciendo un nuevo enfoque prometedor para el tratamiento de la pérdida de peso.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, stylized peptide chain gently entwined with a subtle neuron network shaped like a brain’s hypothalamus, surrounded by calm, healthy animals such as a lean mouse and a minipig peacefully resting, all rendered in soft natural tones of beige, light brown, and muted greens.

articles.title: New toothpaste stops gum disease without killing good bacteria
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a new way to fight gum disease without wiping out the mouth’s helpful bacteria—a major shift from traditional treatments. Instead of killing everything, this targeted approach blocks only the harmful microbes that drive periodontitis, allowing beneficial bacteria to thrive and restore balance naturally.
articles.content:
Periodontitis is a common condition that can affect much more than just oral health. Scientists at Fraunhofer have discovered a compound that specifically blocks the bacteria responsible for this disease while leaving the rest of the oral microbiome intact. This innovation has since been developed into a range of oral care products by the spin-off company PerioTrap.

The human mouth contains more than 700 types of bacteria, but only a small number are linked to periodontitis. These harmful microbes collect in dental plaque, especially along the gum line, where they can trigger inflammation (gingivitis). If left untreated, this inflammation can progress into chronic periodontitis, leading to gum recession and tooth loss.

The risks extend beyond the mouth. When disease-causing bacteria enter the bloodstream, they may play a role in serious conditions such as diabetes, rheumatic disease, arthritis, cardiovascular disease, chronic inflammatory bowel disease, and even Alzheimer's disease.

Why Conventional Treatments Fall Short

Traditional oral care products like alcohol-based mouthwashes and chlorhexidine solutions kill harmful bacteria, but they also wipe out beneficial microbes. After treatment, the oral microbiome has to rebuild itself from scratch. In this process, harmful bacteria such as Porphyromonas gingivalis often regain dominance quickly because they thrive in inflamed gum tissue. Beneficial bacteria grow more slowly, which can lead to an imbalance known as dysbiosis and allow the disease to return.

A New Way to Block Harmful Bacteria

Researchers at the Fraunhofer Institute for Cell Therapy and Immunology IZI in Halle identified a substance that targets harmful pathogens without affecting the rest of the microbial community. This compound, called guanidinoethylbenzylamino imidazopyridine acetate, works by preventing the growth of bacteria like Porphyromonas gingivalis rather than killing them outright.

Stephan Schilling, Head of the Fraunhofer IZI branch Molecular Drug Biochemistry and Therapy Development, explains: "Rather than simply killing gingivitis pathogens, it inhibits their growth. They are unable to exert their toxic effects, so beneficial bacteria can occupy niches that would otherwise be inaccessible to them. In this way, the substance works in harmony with healthy bacteria to gently rebuild and stabilize the microbial balance in the mouth," says Schilling.

From Research Project to Toothpaste

The underlying technology was first developed through an EU-funded research project involving international partners. In 2018, Periotrap Pharmaceuticals GmbH was established in Halle to turn this discovery into practical oral care solutions. Working closely with Fraunhofer IZI and the Fraunhofer Institute for Microstructure of Materials and Systems IMWS, the team created a toothpaste designed to support the oral microbiome.

"The product is designed to prevent periodontitis. Like conventional toothpaste, it also contains abrasives and fluoride to prevent tooth decay," explains Mirko Buchholz, one of the company's founders.

Overcoming Development Challenges

Transforming the compound into a usable ingredient required extensive testing. The final product needed to block harmful bacteria effectively while remaining safe for everyday use. It could not be toxic, enter the bloodstream, or cause discoloration of teeth.

To achieve this, Fraunhofer IZI researchers carried out biochemical and structural studies to better understand how the substance works and to fine-tune the formulation. "This allows us to gain a better understanding of how the substances work and determine the optimum composition of the toothpaste's active ingredients," Schilling explains.

Testing Toothpaste Safety and Effectiveness

Fraunhofer IMWS contributed by evaluating how different formulations interact with teeth and gums. Using advanced tools such as scanning electron microscopy and chemical analysis, researchers examined compatibility and performance in detail.

As Andreas Kiesow, Group Manager Characterization of Medical and Cosmetic Care Products, explains: "Scanning electron microscopy, chemical characterization and quantitative measurements enable us to draw detailed conclusions about a substance's compatibility and function. To put it simply: We ultimately find out whether the toothpaste works or not."

Quality Standards and Future Products

All testing followed Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) standards, ensuring that results meet strict national and international requirements. "Compliance with GLP guidelines was a key element of the project. We didn't just develop a good toothpaste with a new ingredient: we developed a high-quality oral care product of medical-grade standard," says Schilling.

Work on the technology is continuing. In addition to toothpaste, researchers and the PerioTrap team have developed a gel used after professional dental cleanings to block harmful bacteria, support a healthy microbiome, and maintain gum health.

More products are in development, including a mouthwash and other oral care solutions. There is also potential for use in veterinary care, since gum disease in dogs and cats has similar underlying causes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in oral health: a new toothpaste that selectively inhibits harmful bacteria causing gum disease while preserving beneficial oral microbiota. This targeted approach represents a meaningful improvement over traditional treatments, potentially reducing periodontitis and its associated systemic health risks for a broad population. The story provides detailed context on the research, development, testing, and future applications, demonstrating substantial real-world impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: New toothpaste fights gum disease by protecting good bacteria
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gum disease, or periodontitis, is a common problem where harmful bacteria cause inflammation and damage to gums and teeth. Traditional treatments kill both bad and good bacteria, which can upset the natural balance in the mouth.

**What happened** – Scientists at the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany discovered a special compound that stops the growth of harmful bacteria linked to gum disease without harming the helpful bacteria. This discovery led to the creation of a new toothpaste by the company PerioTrap that targets only the bad bacteria, helping to keep the mouth healthy.

**Impact** – This toothpaste is different because it does not destroy all bacteria like usual mouthwashes do. Instead, it blocks just the harmful ones, allowing good bacteria to grow and restore balance naturally. This could lower the risk of gum disease and related health problems like heart disease and diabetes.

**What's next step** – Researchers and PerioTrap are working on more products using the same technology, including a gel used after dental cleanings, a mouthwash, and possibly treatments for pets with gum disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new toothpaste protects good mouth bacteria while stopping harmful ones, offering a safer way to prevent gum disease and improve oral health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau dentifrice combat la maladie des gencives en protégeant les bonnes bactéries
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie des gencives, ou parodontite, est un problème courant où des bactéries nuisibles provoquent une inflammation et des dégâts aux gencives et aux dents. Les traitements traditionnels tuent à la fois les mauvaises et les bonnes bactéries, ce qui peut perturber l'équilibre naturel de la bouche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Institut Fraunhofer en Allemagne ont découvert un composé spécial qui empêche la croissance des bactéries responsables de la maladie des gencives sans nuire aux bactéries utiles. Cette découverte a mené à la création d’un nouveau dentifrice par la société PerioTrap qui cible uniquement les mauvaises bactéries, aidant ainsi à garder la bouche en bonne santé.

**Impact** – Ce dentifrice est différent car il ne détruit pas toutes les bactéries comme les bains de bouche habituels. Il bloque seulement les bactéries nuisibles, permettant aux bonnes bactéries de se développer et de rétablir naturellement l’équilibre. Cela pourrait réduire le risque de maladie des gencives et de problèmes de santé liés comme les maladies cardiaques et le diabète.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs et PerioTrap développent d’autres produits utilisant cette technologie, incluant un gel utilisé après les soins dentaires, un bain de bouche, et possiblement des traitements pour les animaux de compagnie souffrant de maladies des gencives.

**Une phrase clé** – Un nouveau dentifrice protège les bonnes bactéries de la bouche tout en stoppant les mauvaises, offrant une manière plus sûre de prévenir la maladie des gencives et d’améliorer la santé bucco-dentaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva pasta dental combate la enfermedad de las encías protegiendo las bacterias buenas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de las encías, o periodontitis, es un problema común donde bacterias dañinas causan inflamación y daño en las encías y dientes. Los tratamientos tradicionales matan tanto las bacterias malas como las buenas, lo que puede alterar el equilibrio natural en la boca.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Instituto Fraunhofer en Alemania descubrieron un compuesto especial que detiene el crecimiento de las bacterias dañinas relacionadas con la enfermedad de las encías sin afectar las bacterias beneficiosas. Este hallazgo llevó a la creación de una nueva pasta dental por la empresa PerioTrap que solo ataca las bacterias malas, ayudando a mantener la boca sana.

**Impacto** – Esta pasta dental es diferente porque no destruye todas las bacterias como los enjuagues bucales comunes. En cambio, bloquea solo las bacterias dañinas, permitiendo que las buenas crezcan y restauren el equilibrio de forma natural. Esto podría reducir el riesgo de enfermedad de las encías y problemas de salud relacionados como enfermedades del corazón y diabetes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores y PerioTrap están trabajando en más productos con esta tecnología, incluyendo un gel para después de limpiezas dentales, un enjuague bucal y posiblemente tratamientos para mascotas con enfermedad de las encías.

**Una frase clave** – Una nueva pasta dental protege las bacterias buenas de la boca mientras detiene las dañinas, ofreciendo una forma más segura de prevenir la enfermedad de las encías y mejorar la salud oral.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a healthy human mouth cross-section showing vibrant, diverse beneficial bacteria as glowing, gentle orbs peacefully surrounding clean white teeth and pink gums, while a subtle, translucent shield-like barrier selectively blocks shadowy, menacing bacteria near the gum line, all rendered in warm, natural tones with soft light emphasizing balance and protection.

articles.title: Men and women with obesity face very different hidden health risks
articles.summary: New research reveals that obesity affects men and women in surprisingly different ways. Men are more likely to develop harmful abdominal fat and signs of liver stress, while women show higher inflammation and cholesterol levels. These differences could help explain why health risks vary between sexes. Scientists say this could lead to more tailored treatments for obesity.
articles.content:
New findings presented at this year's European Congress on Obesity (ECO) in Istanbul, Turkey (May 12-15) highlight clear differences in how obesity affects men and women. The research shows that risks related to heart health, metabolism, and inflammation do not develop in the same way across sexes. These insights could help doctors design more personalized treatment strategies.

A research team from Dokuz Eylul University in Turkey found that men with obesity are more likely to accumulate abdominal (visceral) fat. This type of fat surrounds internal organs and is strongly linked to serious heart and metabolic conditions. Men in the study also showed higher levels of liver enzymes, which may signal liver damage. Women with obesity, on the other hand, were more likely to experience widespread inflammation and elevated cholesterol levels, both of which raise the risk of heart disease and type 2 diabetes.

"Our findings reveal intriguing differences in the way men and women respond to obesity," said lead author Dr. Zeynep Pekel, from Dokuz Eylul University, Izmir, Turkey. "They show just how important gender-specific research is. Not only are sex differences a powerful player in the pathology and course of obesity, but our results indicate that such differences could be a stepping stone toward finding targeted, sex-based therapies to help in the management of people living with obesity."

A Global Health Challenge With Complex Risks

In 2023, about 1.54 billion adults worldwide were living with metabolic syndrome (around 1 in 3 women and 1 in 4 men). This condition includes a group of major risk factors for cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes, such as abdominal obesity, high cholesterol, high blood pressure, and raised fasting plasma glucose.[1]

Obesity itself is a complex chronic disease that affects the body in many ways. It involves changes in metabolism and inflammation, and these changes can vary widely between individuals. Biological sex plays a role in where fat is stored, how the liver processes nutrients, and how the immune system responds. However, detailed comparisons of these differences between men and women with obesity have been limited.

Study Examines Sex-Based Differences in Obesity

To better understand these patterns, researchers analyzed data from 886 women (average age 45 years) and 248 men (average age 41 years) who were treated at the Obesity Clinic in the Department of Internal Medicine at Dokuz Eylul University Faculty of Medicine between 2024 and 2025.

Participants underwent a range of evaluations. Physical measurements included height, weight, body mass index (BMI), and blood pressure. Blood tests measured lipid levels to assess cardiovascular risk, including total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein (LDL), or "bad" cholesterol, high-density lipoprotein (HDL), or "good" cholesterol, triglycerides, and fasting blood glucose.

Researchers also examined markers related to liver function (alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels), kidney function (creatinine levels), and inflammation. Inflammatory indicators included C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, white blood cell count, and platelet count.

Key Differences in Fat Distribution and Biomarkers

The results showed that men had a slightly higher body mass index (BMI) than women (37.5 vs 36 kg/m²). However, their waist circumference was much larger (120 vs 108cm), and their systolic blood pressure was also higher (128 vs 122 mmHg), both of which are linked to increased risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes (see table in notes to editors).

Men also had significantly higher levels of liver enzymes (ALT and GGT), triglycerides, and creatinine. These findings point to a greater likelihood of liver-related and metabolic complications.

Women, in contrast, had higher total cholesterol (215 vs 203 mg/dL) and LDL or "bad" cholesterol (130 vs 123 mg/dL). They also showed higher levels of inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, and platelet count (see table in notes to editors). These patterns suggest a stronger inflammatory response.

Hormones and Biology Help Explain the Differences

According to Pekel, these differences are likely influenced by hormones, immune system activity, and how fat is distributed in the body. Hormones (especially oestrogen) affect fat storage and the body's inflammatory response. Women typically store more fat beneath the skin and tend to show higher levels of inflammation-related markers such as C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. They also generally have a more active immune response, partly due to genetic factors like the X chromosome.

Men are more likely to store fat around internal organs. This visceral fat is closely associated with metabolic disorders and increases the risk of serious health complications.

"It's still early days and these findings need to be confirmed in other patient groups, but they offer important insight into how obesity may affect men and women differently," said Pekel. "These differences are likely influenced by biological factors such as hormones, immune responses, and fat distribution. Our next steps are to validate these findings in larger populations, better understand the biological processes behind these differences, and explore how these patterns relate to clinical risk."

Study Limitations and Next Steps

The researchers note several limitations. The study is cross-sectional, which means it cannot determine cause and effect and may be influenced by confounding factors or reverse causation. In addition, most participants were adults of Turkish ethnicity, so the findings may not fully apply to other populations. Larger and more diverse studies will be needed to confirm and expand on these results.

Notes

Worldwide trends in metabolic syndrome from 2000 to 2023: a systematic review and modelling analysis | Nature Communications

This research is based on abstract 1854 presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO). All abstracts were reviewed by the congress selection committee. A full paper has not yet been published, but the authors are available to answer questions.
articles.reasoning: The article reports new scientific research revealing significant sex-based differences in obesity-related health risks, which has broad implications for improving personalized treatment strategies for a major global health challenge. The findings provide meaningful insights that could lead to better-targeted therapies, benefiting a large population worldwide affected by obesity and metabolic syndrome. The article is focused, detailed, and highlights a positive step forward in medical understanding and patient care.
articles.personality_title: New study finds men and women face different health risks from obesity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Obesity is a common health problem that increases the risk of heart disease, diabetes, and other illnesses. Scientists know that obesity affects the body’s fat, liver, and inflammation, but they have not fully understood how these effects differ between men and women.

**What happened** – Researchers from Dokuz Eylul University in Turkey studied 1,134 adults with obesity (886 women and 248 men) between 2024 and 2025. They measured body fat, blood pressure, liver health, cholesterol, and inflammation markers. They found that men tend to have more fat around their organs and signs of liver stress, while women have higher cholesterol and inflammation levels.

**Impact** – This research shows clear differences in how obesity affects men and women. Men’s fat around organs is linked to serious heart and liver problems, while women’s higher inflammation and cholesterol increase risks of heart disease and diabetes. Understanding these differences can help doctors create treatments that are better suited to each sex, making care more effective.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study larger and more diverse groups to confirm these findings. They also want to learn more about how hormones and biology cause these differences. This could lead to new, personalized therapies for people with obesity.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Men and women with obesity face different health risks, and recognizing these differences can guide better, personalized treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude révèle que l’obésité présente des risques de santé différents pour les hommes et les femmes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’obésité est un problème de santé courant qui augmente les risques de maladies cardiaques, de diabète et d’autres troubles. Les scientifiques savent que l’obésité affecte la graisse du corps, le foie et l’inflammation, mais ils ne comprenaient pas encore clairement comment ces effets diffèrent entre hommes et femmes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Dokuz Eylul en Turquie ont étudié 1 134 adultes obèses (886 femmes et 248 hommes) entre 2024 et 2025. Ils ont mesuré la graisse corporelle, la tension artérielle, la santé du foie, le cholestérol et les marqueurs d’inflammation. Ils ont découvert que les hommes ont tendance à avoir plus de graisse autour des organes et des signes de stress hépatique, tandis que les femmes ont un taux plus élevé de cholestérol et d’inflammation.

**Impact** – Cette recherche montre des différences claires dans la manière dont l’obésité affecte les hommes et les femmes. La graisse autour des organes chez les hommes est liée à des problèmes graves du cœur et du foie, tandis que l’inflammation et le cholestérol plus élevés chez les femmes augmentent les risques de maladies cardiaques et de diabète. Comprendre ces différences peut aider les médecins à créer des traitements mieux adaptés à chaque sexe, rendant les soins plus efficaces.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’étudier des groupes plus grands et diversifiés pour confirmer ces résultats. Ils veulent aussi mieux comprendre comment les hormones et la biologie expliquent ces différences. Cela pourrait mener à de nouvelles thérapies personnalisées pour les personnes obèses.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Hommes et femmes obèses présentent des risques de santé différents, et reconnaître ces différences peut guider des traitements personnalisés plus efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio revela que hombres y mujeres enfrentan riesgos de salud distintos por la obesidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La obesidad es un problema de salud común que aumenta el riesgo de enfermedades cardíacas, diabetes y otras afecciones. Los científicos saben que la obesidad afecta la grasa corporal, el hígado y la inflamación, pero no entendían bien cómo estos efectos difieren entre hombres y mujeres.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Dokuz Eylul en Turquía estudiaron a 1,134 adultos con obesidad (886 mujeres y 248 hombres) entre 2024 y 2025. Midieron la grasa corporal, la presión arterial, la salud del hígado, el colesterol y los marcadores de inflamación. Encontraron que los hombres tienden a tener más grasa alrededor de los órganos y signos de estrés hepático, mientras que las mujeres tienen niveles más altos de colesterol e inflamación.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra diferencias claras en cómo la obesidad afecta a hombres y mujeres. La grasa alrededor de los órganos en los hombres está relacionada con problemas graves del corazón y el hígado, mientras que la mayor inflamación y colesterol en las mujeres aumentan el riesgo de enfermedades cardíacas y diabetes. Entender estas diferencias puede ayudar a los médicos a crear tratamientos mejor adaptados a cada sexo, haciendo la atención más efectiva.

**Próximos pasos** – Los científicos planean estudiar grupos más grandes y diversos para confirmar estos resultados. También quieren aprender más sobre cómo las hormonas y la biología causan estas diferencias. Esto podría llevar a nuevas terapias personalizadas para personas con obesidad.

**Resumen en una frase** – Hombres y mujeres con obesidad enfrentan riesgos de salud diferentes, y reconocer estas diferencias puede guiar tratamientos personalizados más efectivos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two stylized human silhouettes side by side—one with a glowing, golden abdominal area symbolizing visceral fat and liver health, surrounded by subtle swirling red and orange hues representing metabolic activity; the other silhouette with soft, textured layers of pale pink and cream tones around the body’s surface, accented by delicate floating particles symbolizing inflammation and elevated cholesterol, all set against a gentle, neutral background using natural and simple colors.

articles.title: Scientists were wrong about lifespan. Your genes matter way more than we thought
articles.summary: For years, scientists believed our lifespan was mostly shaped by environment and chance, with genetics playing only a minor role. But a new study from the Weizmann Institute flips that idea on its head, revealing that genes may actually account for about half of the differences in how long people live. By analyzing massive twin datasets—including twins raised apart—and using innovative simulations to filter out deaths from accidents and other external causes, researchers uncovered a hidden genetic influence that had been masked for decades.
articles.content:
What influences how long we live, and how much of that is written in our genes? For many years, scientists believed genetics played only a modest role. Earlier estimates suggested that inherited factors explained about 20 to 25 percent of lifespan differences, and some large studies even placed the number below 10 percent.

A new study from the Weizmann Institute of Science, published in Science, challenges that long-standing view. The researchers report that genetics may account for roughly half of the variation in human lifespan, which is at least double previous estimates. The study was led by Ben Shenhar from the lab of Prof. Uri Alon in Weizmann's Molecular Cell Biology Department.

"For many years, lifespan was attributed mainly to non-genetic factors, fueling skepticism about genetic determinants of longevity."

Why Earlier Estimates Missed the Mark

To reach this conclusion, the team analyzed three extensive twin databases from Sweden and Denmark. For the first time in this type of research, they also included data from twins who were raised apart. This allowed them to better separate genetic influences from environmental ones.

The researchers found that earlier estimates were skewed by what scientists call extrinsic mortality. This includes deaths caused by accidents, infections, and environmental factors. Because older datasets did not include detailed causes of death, it was not possible to separate these external influences from deaths linked to biological aging.

To overcome this challenge, the team created a new analytical approach. They used mathematical models along with simulations of virtual twins to distinguish deaths caused by aging from those due to external factors. By filtering out these outside influences, the researchers uncovered a much stronger genetic signal than previously recognized. The findings align with what is seen in other complex human traits and in animal studies.

Up to age 80, the risk of dying from dementia shows a heritability of about 70 percent – far higher than that of cancer or heart disease.

Implications for Aging Research and Medicine

These results could reshape how scientists think about aging and longevity. If genetics plays a larger role than assumed, it strengthens the case for searching for specific genes that influence lifespan.

"For many years, human lifespan was thought to be shaped almost entirely by non-genetic factors, which led to considerable skepticism about the role of genetics in aging and about the feasibility of identifying genetic determinants of longevity," says Shenhar. "By contrast, if heritability is high, as we have shown, this creates an incentive to search for gene variants that extend lifespan, in order to understand the biology of aging and, potentially, to address it therapeutically."

Research Support and Funding

Prof. Uri Alon's research is supported by the Sagol Institute for Longevity Research; the Knell Family Institute for Artificial Intelligence; the Moross Integrated Cancer Center; the David and Fela Shapell Family Center for Genetic Disorders Research; the Zuckerman STEM Leadership Program; and the Rising Tide Foundation.

Prof. Alon is the incumbent of the Abisch-Frenkel Professorial Chair.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough revealing that genetics play a much larger role in human lifespan than previously thought, doubling earlier estimates. This finding has broad implications for aging research and medicine, potentially guiding new therapeutic approaches to extend healthy lifespan. The study is well-supported by extensive data and innovative methods, providing substantial context and significance for the general public.
articles.personality_title: New study shows genes play a bigger role in how long we live
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, scientists believed that how long people live mostly depends on their environment and luck, with genes playing only a small part. Earlier studies suggested that genes explained about 10 to 25 percent of lifespan differences.

**What happened** – Researchers from the Weizmann Institute in Israel studied large groups of twins from Sweden and Denmark, including twins raised apart. They used new methods to separate deaths caused by accidents or infections from those caused by aging. This allowed them to see that genes actually explain about half of the differences in how long people live.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it shows genes have a much stronger influence on lifespan than scientists thought before. It means that studying genes could help us understand aging better and possibly find ways to live healthier, longer lives. For example, the study found that the risk of dying from dementia is strongly linked to genetics.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely focus more on finding the specific genes that affect how long people live. This could lead to new treatments or medicines that target aging and related diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research reveals that genes influence about half of how long people live, doubling previous estimates and opening new paths for aging research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude montre que les gènes jouent un rôle plus important dans la durée de vie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les scientifiques ont pensé que la durée de vie dépendait surtout de l'environnement et de la chance, les gènes jouant un rôle mineur. Les études précédentes estimaient que les gènes expliquaient environ 10 à 25 % des différences de durée de vie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Institut Weizmann en Israël ont étudié de grands groupes de jumeaux en Suède et au Danemark, y compris des jumeaux élevés séparément. Ils ont utilisé de nouvelles méthodes pour distinguer les décès dus aux accidents ou infections de ceux liés au vieillissement. Ils ont ainsi découvert que les gènes expliquent en réalité environ la moitié des différences de durée de vie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les gènes ont une influence beaucoup plus forte sur la durée de vie que ce que l’on pensait auparavant. Cela signifie que l’étude des gènes pourrait aider à mieux comprendre le vieillissement et peut-être à vivre plus longtemps en meilleure santé. Par exemple, l’étude a montré que le risque de mourir de démence est fortement lié à la génétique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement se concentrer davantage sur la recherche des gènes spécifiques qui influencent la durée de vie. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux traitements ciblant le vieillissement et les maladies associées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche révèle que les gènes influencent environ la moitié de la durée de vie, doublant les estimations précédentes et ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la recherche sur le vieillissement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio muestra que los genes juegan un papel más importante en la duración de la vida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que cuánto vive una persona dependía principalmente del ambiente y la suerte, y que los genes tenían un papel pequeño. Estudios anteriores decían que los genes explicaban entre 10 y 25 % de las diferencias en la duración de vida.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Instituto Weizmann en Israel estudiaron grandes grupos de gemelos en Suecia y Dinamarca, incluyendo gemelos criados por separado. Usaron métodos nuevos para separar las muertes causadas por accidentes o infecciones de las causadas por el envejecimiento. Así descubrieron que los genes explican cerca de la mitad de las diferencias en cuánto vive la gente.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que los genes influyen mucho más en la duración de vida de lo que se pensaba. Esto significa que estudiar los genes podría ayudar a entender mejor el envejecimiento y quizás encontrar formas de vivir más tiempo y con mejor salud. Por ejemplo, encontraron que el riesgo de morir por demencia está muy ligado a la genética.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente se enfocarán más en encontrar los genes específicos que afectan la duración de la vida. Esto podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para el envejecimiento y enfermedades relacionadas.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva investigación revela que los genes influyen en cerca de la mitad de la duración de la vida, doblando las estimaciones anteriores y abriendo nuevas vías para la investigación del envejecimiento.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of two intertwined, stylized DNA double helixes glowing softly side by side, surrounded by abstract twin silhouettes made of gentle, flowing lines that subtly mirror each other, set against a calm, natural background with muted earth and sky colors, symbolizing the deep genetic connection influencing human lifespan.
